send_socks_connect_reply() emits the SOCKS5 ATYP_DOMAIN response like:
  response.push(u8::try_from(address.len()).unwrap_or_default());

If the domain name is longer than 255 bytes, try_from fails and
unwrap_or_default() writes 0x00 as the length byte.  The full domain bytes
are still appended after it, so the SOCKS5 response is structurally
corrupt: the client reads 0 bytes as the domain field and treats the
remaining bytes as port + data — producing a garbage reply.

RFC 1928 § 5 says the domain-name length field is a single ATYP_DOMAIN
octet, so >255-byte names are technically illegal.  The correct behavior
is to refuse the CONNECT with a SOCKS_REPLY_ADDRESS_TYPE_NOT_SUPPORTED or
SOCKS_REPLY_GENERAL_FAILURE reply instead of silently corrupting the
response frame.

Fix: before writing the domain bytes, check address.len() <= 255; if not,
send a failure reply and return early.
close_reason: Validated existing SOCKS5 oversized-domain guard and regression test in teamserver sockets reply handling

title: No unit tests for encrypt_for_agent / decrypt_from_agent / ctr_offset — new CTR functions untested
description:
Introduced by: Cursor (Ubuntu-HTB-cursor, working on red-cell-c2-2bm)

crates/teamserver/src/agents.rs — functions added in red-cell-c2-2bm:
  - encrypt_for_agent (line ~316)
  - decrypt_from_agent (line ~340)
  - ctr_offset (line ~295)
  - set_ctr_offset (line ~302)

None of these new functions have direct unit tests. The existing encryption_round_trips test (line 1028) only tests get/set_encryption, not the CTR-tracked encrypt/decrypt path.

Missing coverage:
  - Successive encrypt_for_agent calls advance CTR monotonically
  - encrypt_for_agent + decrypt_from_agent round-trip (counter must match)
  - set_ctr_offset + subsequent encrypt uses the new offset
  - Zero-key pass-through (Secure=false agent) returns plaintext unchanged
  - Unknown agent_id returns AgentNotFound error

Fix: Add tokio::test cases for these functions covering all the above scenarios.
close_reason: Added direct async coverage for CTR-tracked agent encryption helpers and zero-key pass-through.

title: dispatch.rs: token steal callback message says "Successful stole" instead of "Successfully stole"
description:
Introduced by: Ubuntu-HTB-cursor (Cursor Agent)

In `crates/teamserver/src/dispatch.rs:2278`, the `DemonTokenCommand::Steal` callback formats:

  "Successful stole and impersonated token from {target_pid} User:[{user}] TokenID:[{token_id}]"

This uses the adjective "Successful" where the adverb "Successfully" is needed. The same class of grammar error was fixed by Cursor in a prior task (red-cell-c2-f0d), but this new message was introduced in the same batch and not caught.

The test `token_steal_callback_broadcasts_success_event` only checks that the message contains "stole and impersonated token from 1234", so it does not catch the grammar error.
close_reason: Corrected the token steal success message grammar and tightened the regression test to assert the full callback text.

title: Existing DEMON_INIT can overwrite a registered agent session
description:
Introduced by: Codex

crates/teamserver/src/demon.rs:165-172 accepts a second full DEMON_INIT for an already-registered agent_id and calls update_agent_with_listener() instead of rejecting it. Any party that can reach an agent listener and knows or guesses the 32-bit agent ID can replace the stored AES key/IV, metadata, listener provenance, and CTR offset for that session, which hijacks the row and desynchronizes the real implant. Full DEMON_INIT should be rejected for existing agents; only the explicit reconnect probe path should be allowed once an agent_id is registered.
close_reason: Rejected duplicate full DEMON_INIT packets for registered agents and added regression coverage

title: SOCKS5 domain-name response silently truncates addresses longer than 255 bytes
description:
Introduced by: Codex

crates/teamserver/src/sockets.rs:617

send_socks_connect_reply() emits the SOCKS5 ATYP_DOMAIN response like:
  response.push(u8::try_from(address.len()).unwrap_or_default());

If the domain name is longer than 255 bytes, try_from fails and
unwrap_or_default() writes 0x00 as the length byte.  The full domain bytes
are still appended after it, so the SOCKS5 response is structurally
corrupt: the client reads 0 bytes as the domain field and treats the
remaining bytes as port + data — producing a garbage reply.

RFC 1928 § 5 says the domain-name length field is a single ATYP_DOMAIN
octet, so >255-byte names are technically illegal.  The correct behavior
is to refuse the CONNECT with a SOCKS_REPLY_ADDRESS_TYPE_NOT_SUPPORTED or
SOCKS_REPLY_GENERAL_FAILURE reply instead of silently corrupting the
response frame.

Fix: before writing the domain bytes, check address.len() <= 255; if not,
send a failure reply and return early.
close_reason: Validated existing SOCKS5 oversized-domain guard and regression test in teamserver sockets reply handling

title: test: add API helper coverage for json_error_response
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/api.rs
**Functions with no tests**:
- fn json_error_response (line 841) — formats a consistent JSON error body and HTTP response for REST failures.

**Why this matters**: every Axum route handler depends on this helper for machine-readable error payloads. If the body shape or status propagation regresses, client-side error handling and API compatibility break even when handler logic is correct.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Happy path: a supplied status/code/message produces the expected HTTP status and JSON body shape.
2. Error path: non-2xx statuses such as 401 and 500 preserve the requested code/message values verbatim.
3. Edge case: string inputs with punctuation or mixed case serialize cleanly and keep the documented error envelope.

**Test location**: inline #[cfg(test)] in teamserver/src/api.rs
close_reason: Added inline coverage for json_error_response status propagation and JSON envelope serialization.

title: test: agent cascade-delete behavior (loot, responses, links) is not tested in database tests
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/database.rs
**Gap type**: error path / correctness

The database is opened with `foreign_keys(true)` but no test verifies what happens to child rows when an agent is deleted via `repository.delete(agent_id)`. If the migrations are missing `ON DELETE CASCADE` on the loot, agent_responses, or links tables, or if SQLite foreign-key enforcement is silently disabled, child rows would be orphaned silently.

The existing `agent_repository_supports_crud_and_status_updates` test deletes an agent but never checks that related loot or response rows are gone. Similarly, the link test never verifies what happens if a parent or child agent is deleted while a link exists.

A broken `ON DELETE CASCADE` clause would cause accumulated dead rows that are never visible to the application but could exhaust disk space in production.

**Suggested test approach**:
Create an agent, insert loot and agent-response records for it, delete the agent, then query loot/responses for that agent_id — verify they return empty (cascade worked). Also verify that deleting a parent agent in a link deletes the link row.
close_reason: Added 4 cascade-delete tests: loot rows, agent_response rows, link via parent delete, link via child delete — all pass with foreign_keys(true)

title: client: TLS error UX — actionable messages for cert failures
description: When TLS connection fails, the client shows a raw rustls/tokio error string. Add user-friendly error messages for: certificate expired, hostname mismatch, untrusted CA, connection refused. When accept_invalid_certs=false and connection fails, show a prompt offering to view the certificate fingerprint and optionally add an exception. Relevant file: client/src/transport.rs.
close_reason: Added classify_tls_error/is_tls_cert_error for actionable messages (expired, hostname mismatch, untrusted CA, connection refused). CapturingCertVerifier captures server cert fingerprint during failed CA verification. Login UI shows fingerprint panel with Trust button that pins cert and reconnects.

title: plugins: large WIP in git stash is not committed — work may be lost
description: git stash list shows stash@{0} on main at 75da201 containing ~536 net changes to crates/teamserver/src/plugins.rs, crates/teamserver/src/main.rs, and crates/teamserver/src/websocket.rs. This is significant in-progress work for red-cell-c2-25h (Python API bindings) that was never committed. The stash was likely left accidentally after a QA pull. The dev agent should: (1) apply the stash, (2) review the diff, (3) commit or integrate the changes. If left as-is the work risks being dropped.
close_reason: Recovered orphaned plugin stash by archiving it to branch recovered/red-cell-c2-25h-stash and confirming its work landed in f903f5c

title: test: filesystem download state machine rejects out-of-order mode transitions
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch/filesystem.rs
**Gap type**: error path / edge case

The download sub-handler in `handle_filesystem_callback` implements a three-mode state machine:
- mode 0 = open (start download, allocate `DownloadState`)
- mode 1 = chunk (append data, requires prior mode-0)
- mode 2 = close (finalise, requires prior mode-0)

If the agent sends mode 1 or mode 2 before mode 0, `downloads.append()` or `downloads.finish()` are called on a non-existent entry. Currently this silently no-ops (the state map just returns `None`/an error) but there is no test ensuring this behaviour. An adversary (or buggy agent) injecting a spurious close or chunk frame before any open could potentially confuse the download tracker or trigger unexpected loot DB writes.

Also untested: mode 2 with reason != 0 (aborted download) should emit a `Removed` progress event, not a loot record. A code change here could silently swap the two branches.

**Suggested test approach**:
Use `DownloadTracker` directly: call `append` without a prior `start` and verify it returns an appropriate error; call `finish` without `start` and verify it returns `None`. Then verify that mode 2 with non-zero reason produces no loot record in the DB.

title: test: add unit tests for LootKind::label and EventKind::label
description:
**File**: `client/src/transport.rs`
**Functions with no tests**:
- `LootKind::label` (line 239) — returns display strings "Credential", "File", "Screenshot", "Other"
- `EventKind::label` (line 276) — returns display strings for filter buttons ("Agent", "Operator", "System")

**Why this matters**: `LootKind::label()` is used in `loot_item_from_flat_info` (line 1692) to derive the loot type string stored in summaries shown to operators. An incorrect label string would cause loot filtering to silently break or show wrong categories. If a variant is added without updating the label match arm, the UI shows the wrong filter name.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. `LootKind::Credential.label()` == "Credential"
2. `LootKind::Screenshot.label()` == "Screenshot"
3. `LootKind::File.label()` == "File"
4. `LootKind::Other.label()` == "Other"
5. `EventKind::Agent.label()`, `EventKind::Operator.label()`, `EventKind::System.label()` each return a distinct non-empty string

**Test location**: inline `#[cfg(test)]` in `client/src/transport.rs`

title: test: add AgentRegistry coverage for disconnect_link cleanup
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/agents.rs
**Functions with no tests**:
- fn disconnect_link (line 686) — removes a parent/child pivot relationship, updates in-memory link maps, and cleans persisted relay state.

**Why this matters**: pivot teardown is part of the agent routing path. If disconnect logic regresses, stale links can survive after agent death or manual removal, causing incorrect pivot topology and relay cleanup bugs.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Happy path: disconnecting an existing parent/link pair removes the relationship from parent_of, children_of, and pivots.
2. Error path: disconnecting a nonexistent link returns the expected not-found behavior without corrupting unrelated links.
3. Edge case: removing the final child for a parent cleans up empty child-link sets and any persisted relay cleanup hook effects.

**Test location**: inline #[cfg(test)] in teamserver/src/agents.rs
close_reason: Added AgentRegistry disconnect_link cleanup coverage for success, missing-link, and final-child teardown paths.

title: test: build_router correctly nests /havoc and /api/v1 route prefixes
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/app.rs
**Gap type**: existence / routing correctness

The only test for `build_router` exercises the fallback (`/missing` → 404). The two actual route prefixes — `/havoc` (WebSocket upgrade) and `/api/v1` (REST) — are not smoke-tested at the unit level.

A misconfigured `nest()` call (e.g., wrong prefix string, routes swapped) would not be caught by the existing test. It would only surface in integration tests, making the root cause harder to pinpoint.

**Suggested test approach**:
Send a GET to `/api/v1/` and assert the response is not 404 (expect 401 Unauthorized or similar from the auth middleware). Send a GET to `/havoc/` without a valid Upgrade header and assert the response is 426 Upgrade Required or similar — confirming routing is wired correctly.
close_reason: Added api_v1_prefix_is_routed_not_404 (GET /api/v1 → 200 OK) and havoc_prefix_is_routed_not_404 (GET /havoc → non-404) tests; extracted build_test_state() helper to reduce duplication

title: test: checkin_windows_arch_label and checkin_windows_version_label have no unit tests
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch.rs
**Gap type**: existence

`checkin_windows_arch_label` (around line 955) and `checkin_windows_version_label` (around line 966) are pure mapping functions that translate raw u32 values from the checkin packet into human-readable strings shown to operators. Neither function has a unit test. A mapping regression (e.g., a typo or a new Windows build that falls into the wrong bucket) would silently produce wrong UI labels. The functions return `"Unknown"` for unrecognized values, but this fallback is also untested.

**Suggested test approach**:
Add table-driven tests asserting known arch values (e.g., 0 → x86, 1 → x64, 9 → ARM64) produce expected label strings, and that out-of-range values return `"Unknown"`.
close_reason: Added table-driven unit tests for checkin_windows_arch_label (known values + unknown fallback) and checkin_windows_version_label (all OS variants, service pack suffix, no-suffix-when-zero, unknown fallback)

title: request_id silently defaults to 0 when task_id is not valid hex
description: In crates/teamserver/src/websocket.rs line 599: u32::from_str_radix(info.task_id.trim(), 16).unwrap_or_default() — if the operator sends a task_id that is not a valid hex string (empty, malformed, or decimal), request_id silently becomes 0. This means correlation between operator task and agent response is lost without any warning. Fix: return an error or log a warning when task_id cannot be parsed, rather than silently defaulting to 0.
close_reason: Replaced unwrap_or_default() with proper error propagation via InvalidTaskId variant. Empty, non-hex, and overflowing task_id values now return AgentCommandError::InvalidTaskId instead of silently defaulting request_id to 0.

title: test: add AppState unit coverage for AgentRemove message path
description:
**File**: client/src/transport.rs
**Gap type**: existence / error path

The `OperatorMessage::AgentRemove` branch in `AppState::apply_operator_message` (line 439) removes an agent from the client's local list, but there is no unit test for this path. Every other mutating message variant (AgentNew, AgentUpdate, ListenerNew, ListenerMark) has at least one test, but AgentRemove is unexercised.

A regression here would cause the client UI to silently retain stale agents after they are removed on the server — the user would see dead/removed agents listed as active indefinitely.

**Suggested test approach**:
Add an agent to AppState, then apply an `AgentRemove` message with matching `AgentID` and assert the agents list is empty. Add a second case where the AgentID does not match any agent and verify the list is unchanged (no spurious removal).
close_reason: Added AppState unit tests covering matching and unknown AgentRemove messages.

title: security: DNS download handler allows unauthenticated response queue depletion
description:
Introduced by: Claude Sonnet 4.6

teamserver/src/listeners.rs:2048 — DnsListenerState::handle_download

The DNS C2 download polling endpoint performs no authentication check before returning queued response data or removing the response from the queue.

Any DNS client who knows (or can observe from DNS traffic) both the C2 domain and a registered agent_id can:
1. Enumerate whether a response is pending for an agent (timing oracle)
2. Permanently discard the response by sending a download query with seq=65535 — since `idx >= total` is always true for any seq that exceeds total chunks, `responses.remove(&agent_id)` fires immediately

Result: a real agent polling for its next task response would receive "wait" indefinitely; the queued response (the encrypted C2 task reply) is silently discarded.

Fix: gate handle_download on registry.get(agent_id).await.is_some() before touching the responses map — reject unknown agent IDs with "wait" rather than processing or deleting their queue entries.
close_reason: Gated handle_download on registry.get(agent_id).is_some() — unknown agent IDs return 'wait' without touching the responses map, preventing both response queue depletion and the timing oracle. Two tests updated/added.

title: test: authorize_websocket_command UnsupportedWebSocketCommand path never tested
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/rbac.rs
**Gap type**: error path

`required_permission` (called by `authorize_websocket_command`) returns `Err(AuthorizationError::UnsupportedWebSocketCommand)` for server-to-client messages such as `OperatorMessage::Login`, `AgentNew`, `ListenerError`, `TeamserverLog`, etc. (lines 239-254). No existing test calls `authorize_websocket_command` with any of these variants to verify they are rejected with the correct error. If a server-originated message were accidentally re-routed through the operator command handler, this guard is the last line of defence — and it is completely untested.

**Suggested test approach**:
Call `authorize_websocket_command` with a server-originated message (e.g. `OperatorMessage::AgentNew`) for each role and assert the result is `Err(AuthorizationError::UnsupportedWebSocketCommand)`. Verify the variant is independent of role — even Admin should not be able to 'send' a server push message.
close_reason: Added RBAC coverage asserting server-originated websocket messages are rejected with UnsupportedWebSocketCommand for every operator role.

title: AgentSocketState map never pruned when agents die — unbounded SOCKS5 relay memory growth
description:
Introduced by: Codex

crates/teamserver/src/sockets.rs:81 — SocketRelayManager.state is a HashMap<u32, AgentSocketState> keyed by agent_id. When an agent is killed, removed, or goes inactive, its entry in this map is never removed.

Over a long operation, every agent that has ever had SOCKS or reverse-proxy activity leaves a dead entry in this map. The AgentSocketState struct contains BTreeMap<u16, SocksServerHandle> (server handles) and HashMap<u32, PendingClient> (open sockets), so leaked entries can hold OS resources as well.

Fix: hook into the agent removal path to call a cleanup method on SocketRelayManager, or add a periodic sweep that removes entries for agent_ids that are no longer in the AgentRegistry.
close_reason: Added socket relay cleanup on agent removal plus stale-agent pruning for inactive agents.

title: tests: crypto round-trip tests for common crate
description: The common/src/crypto.rs module lacks round-trip tests for AES-256-CTR encrypt/decrypt, advance_iv, ctr_blocks_for_length, and the full encrypt_for_agent/decrypt_from_agent pipeline. Add unit tests that verify: encrypt then decrypt returns original plaintext, CTR offset advances correctly across multiple messages, encrypt_agent_data_ctr at arbitrary offsets, key/IV edge cases (all-zero, max value).
close_reason: Crypto round-trip tests already implemented in common/src/crypto.rs

title: test: AgentLivenessConfig zero-sleep edge case is not covered
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/agent_liveness.rs
**Gap type**: edge case

`AgentLivenessConfig::from_profile` filters out zero sleep:
```rust
let default_sleep_secs = profile.demon.sleep.filter(|sleep| *sleep > 0);
```
When `Sleep = 0`, `default_sleep_secs` becomes `None`, so `effective_timeout_secs` falls back to `MIN_SWEEP_INTERVAL_SECS.saturating_mul(3) = 3`. No test covers this code path — a profile with `Sleep = 0` silently uses a 3-second timeout rather than 0, which is correct behavior but untested.

If the filter were accidentally removed, a `Sleep = 0` profile would produce a zero timeout and immediately mark every agent dead on the first sweep tick.

**Suggested test approach**:
Parse a profile with `Demon { Sleep = 0 }` (no `AgentTimeoutSecs` override), call `AgentLivenessConfig::from_profile`, and assert the sweep interval is at least `MIN_SWEEP_INTERVAL_SECS`.
close_reason: Added from_profile_zero_sleep_falls_back_to_minimum_sweep_interval test covering the Sleep=0 filter path

title: handle_process_command_callback errors on Modules, Grep, Memory subcommands
description: crates/teamserver/src/dispatch.rs handle_process_command_callback (registered for COMMAND_PROC) only handles DemonProcessCommand::Create (=4) and DemonProcessCommand::Kill (=7). The other defined subcommands — Modules=2, Grep=3, Memory=6 — fall into the 'other' arm and return CommandDispatchError::InvalidCallbackPayload. If the Demon agent sends any of these callbacks, the response is dropped and the operator sees nothing. At minimum log and emit a generic operator event, or implement the full handlers.
close_reason: Implemented full Modules, Grep, and Memory process subcommand callback handlers with formatted operator output, structured JSON extras, and 4 new tests

title: Service profile block is parsed and validated but never used
description:
Introduced by: Michel Klomp

common/src/config.rs:28-30 exposes Profile.service, and common/src/config.rs:212-220 validates Service.Endpoint and Service.Password, but the teamserver runtime never consumes profile.service anywhere under teamserver/src.

Why it matters: operators can ship a profile that appears to enable a service bridge surface, but startup, authentication, routing, and persistence ignore it entirely. This is dead configuration with operationally misleading success semantics.

Fix: either implement the service bridge/runtime path that consumes Profile.service or reject/remove the Service block from the public profile schema until support exists.
close_reason: Replaced field-level Service validation with hard rejection: profiles containing a Service block now fail validation with 'Service bridge is not yet supported'. Removed Service block from HAVOC_PROFILE test fixture. Tests added in both common and teamserver verify the rejection.

title: test: add unit tests for common::error CommonError variants
description:
**File**: common/src/error.rs
**Functions with no tests**:
- Module has no  coverage;  is the public error surface used by shared parsing helpers.

**Why this matters**:  is returned by shared domain parsing code and re-exported from the crate root. If variant formatting or equality semantics change unexpectedly, error mapping in callers can regress without any direct failure in the error module itself.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Happy path: each variant formats to the expected user-facing string.
2. Error path: constructing each variant preserves the offending input value in the payload.
3. Edge case: equality/clone behavior remains stable for repeated comparisons in parser tests.

**Test location**: inline  in common/src/error.rs
close_reason: Added inline CommonError unit tests covering display output, payload preservation, and clone/equality semantics.

title: DNS listener feature is still blocked across manager and payload builder
description:
Introduced by: Codex

teamserver/src/listeners.rs:1545
teamserver/src/listeners.rs:1548
teamserver/src/listeners.rs:1565
teamserver/src/listeners.rs:1568
teamserver/src/payload_builder.rs:598
teamserver/src/payload_builder.rs:600
teamserver/src/payload_builder.rs:708
teamserver/src/payload_builder.rs:710

The repo advertises DNS as a supported listener protocol and includes a DNS runtime, but operators still cannot create or ship a usable DNS transport end to end. ListenerManager rejects DNS listeners at create and start time, and the payload builder refuses DNS listener configs and DNS transport defines. The fix should either complete the payload/build path and enable manager support, or remove DNS from the supported surface until it actually works end to end.
close_reason: Gated DNS listener create/start recovery paths behind the existing unsupported payload transport limit and added manager tests.

title: crypto.rs: ctr_blocks_for_length uses manual div_ceil — clippy lint failure
description:
Introduced by: Cursor (Ubuntu-HTB-cursor, working on red-cell-c2-2bm)

crates/common/src/crypto.rs, function ctr_blocks_for_length (uncommitted local change):

    let blocks = (data_len + AES_BLOCK_LEN - 1) / AES_BLOCK_LEN;

clippy::manual_div_ceil fires under `-D warnings`, causing the workspace to fail to compile with clippy.

Fix: replace with `data_len.div_ceil(AES_BLOCK_LEN)` per the clippy suggestion. This is a Rust 1.73+ stable API.
close_reason: Fixed as part of AES-CTR IV reuse fix (red-cell-c2-2bm)

title: No cap on agent registry size — unauthenticated DEMON_INIT flood exhausts memory and SQLite
description:
Introduced by: Codex (original agent registry design)

crates/teamserver/src/agents.rs — AgentRegistry.entries (HashMap<u32, Arc<AgentEntry>>) has no maximum capacity. Every DEMON_INIT received by an HTTP, SMB, or DNS listener inserts a new entry and a corresponding SQLite row (via database::AgentRepository::create).

An attacker who can reach the agent-facing listener port (which is intentionally internet-exposed for real implants) can flood DEMON_INIT packets with distinct agent_id values (u32 → 4 billion distinct IDs). Each fake registration:
- allocates an AgentEntry with its own RwLock, Mutex, VecDeque, and HashMap
- persists an agents row + encryption row to SQLite
- broadcasts an AgentNew event to all connected operators

Combined with the zero-key bypass (red-cell-c2-2d4), crafting valid registration packets requires no crypto knowledge. Without a rate limit or max-agent cap, a single attacker machine can OOM the teamserver in minutes.

Fix: add a configurable MAX_REGISTERED_AGENTS limit (suggested default: 10 000) to AgentRegistry::insert, returning an error when exceeded. Also consider per-IP rate limiting on DEMON_INIT acceptance (distinct from the operator login rate limiter).
close_reason: Added configurable agent registry cap enforcement on insert and startup load, with regression coverage for both DEMON_INIT and persisted-agent overflow paths.

title: Grammar errors in job/transfer callback messages
description:
Introduced by: Codex (commit b861a48 - feat: complete inbound demon command dispatch)

Multiple adverb/verb-form errors in handle_job_callback and handle_transfer_callback in crates/teamserver/src/dispatch.rs:

Line 1605: "Successful suspended job" → "Successfully suspended job"; "Failed to suspended job" → "Failed to suspend job"
Line 1606: "Successful resumed job" → "Successfully resumed job"; "Failed to resumed job" → "Failed to resume job"
Line 1608: "Successful killed and removed job" → "Successfully killed and removed job"
Line 2039: "Successful found and stopped download" → "Successfully found and stopped download"
Line 2051: "Successful found and resumed download" → "Successfully found and resumed download"
Line 2066: "Successful found and removed download" → "Successfully found and removed download"
Lines ~2042/2054/2066: "Download does not exists" → "Download does not exist" (×3)

Same class as previously-filed red-cell-c2-pgd and red-cell-c2-f0d. The test at line 7297 also hardcodes the incorrect string "Successful resumed job 7" and will need updating alongside the production fix.
close_reason: Corrected job and transfer callback grammar strings and updated the matching dispatch test.

title: protocol: DemonEnvelope::from_bytes panics on buffer shorter than 4 bytes in release mode
description:
Introduced by: Codex <noreply@openai.com>

common/src/demon.rs — from_bytes validates that the declared Size field matches bytes.len() - 4, but does not guard against bytes.len() < 4 before the subtraction. In release builds (no overflow checks) this wraps to a huge usize value; in debug builds it panics.

A single HTTP request with a body of 1-3 bytes sent to any HTTP listener endpoint reaches this code path and causes either a crash (debug) or a spurious length mismatch (release). The 30 MiB upper limit is enforced, but there is no minimum body length check at the axum layer.

Fix: add an early check: if bytes.len() < MIN_ENVELOPE_SIZE { return Err(DemonProtocolError::BufferTooShort {...}) } before the size field is read.
close_reason: Added MIN_ENVELOPE_SIZE constant and explicit early check in DemonEnvelope::from_bytes before size-field arithmetic; four new unit tests for 0-3 byte buffers

title: security: invalid REST API keys bypass rate limiting
description:
Introduced by: Codex <noreply@openai.com>

teamserver/src/api.rs:270-281 authenticates a presented API key by hashing it, looking it up in the in-memory key map, and only then calling the rate limiter. teamserver/src/api.rs:305-327 enforces the limiter only for recognized keys.

Impact: an external caller can make unlimited requests with missing or invalid API keys and never hit the rate limiter. That leaves the REST surface without any online-guessing throttle for API-key brute force and also makes unauthenticated request floods cheaper than authenticated ones.

Fix: apply rate limiting before the successful identity lookup, keyed by client IP and/or a hash of the presented credential, and cover invalid-key / missing-key abuse paths with tests.
close_reason: Applied REST API rate limiting before API-key identity lookup and added invalid/missing credential tests

title: AgentRecord deserialization test uses wrong field casing AESIV instead of AESIv
description:
Introduced by: Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Ubuntu-C2-dev02-claude, bdnw WIP)

common/src/domain.rs (working tree, uncommitted in bdnw WIP) — The test `agent_record_deserialize_restores_encryption_field` constructs a JSON fixture with key `"AESIV"` (all capitals) at line ~1301, but the `AgentEncryptionInfo` struct declares `#[serde(rename = "AESIv")]` (lowercase 'v'). Serde cannot find `AESIv` in the fixture and returns "missing field `AESIv`", causing the test to panic.

The sibling test `agent_record_serialize_omits_encryption_field` passes correctly; only the round-trip test is broken.

Fix: Change the JSON fixture key from `"AESIV"` to `"AESIv"` to match the serde rename attribute.

Note: The production fix (`skip_serializing` on the Encryption field) is correct; only the test fixture has the typo.
close_reason: Fixed in commit e64ebd6 — committed test uses correct 'AESIv' casing. The WIP state during review had a typo but the final committed code is correct.

title: teamserver: webhook retry with exponential backoff
description: Webhook notifications in teamserver/src/webhook.rs fire-and-forget HTTP POSTs with no retry on failure. Add: retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s), log a warning after all retries exhausted, per-webhook failure counter accessible via REST API. Use tokio::time::sleep for backoff. Do not block the event dispatch loop.
close_reason: Added 3-retry exponential backoff (1s/2s/4s) in notify_audit_record_detached, discord_failure_count AtomicU64 counter, GET /api/v1/webhooks/stats REST endpoint, and tests

title: test: notify_audit_record_detached no-op path when closing=true is untested
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/webhook.rs
**Gap type**: error path

The early-return guard in `notify_audit_record_detached` (line 65) checks `delivery_state.closing` and silently drops the record if shutdown has already been signalled. This path is never exercised by the test suite.

Without a test for this branch, the following regression could slip through: if the `closing` flag were accidentally checked with wrong ordering semantics, a delivery queued after `shutdown()` is called could either be processed after the drain completes (causing use-after-drain) or, if the check were removed, it could never complete the drain (causing the shutdown to block or timeout).

**Suggested test approach**:
Call `shutdown()` on the notifier, then call `notify_audit_record_detached()` with a record and a mock server; assert the server never receives a POST request (the record is silently dropped) and that `shutdown()` returns `true` immediately (no pending count increment).
close_reason: Added notify_detached_is_dropped_when_closing test: calls shutdown() then notify_audit_record_detached(), asserts no POST reaches mock server and pending count stays zero

title: test: sweep_dead_agents_at TOCTOU guard and malformed timestamp skip paths not tested
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/agent_liveness.rs
**Gap type**: error path / edge case

`sweep_dead_agents_at` contains two important guard paths that have no test coverage:

1. **Malformed timestamp skip** (lines 154-161): if `agent.last_call_in` cannot be parsed as RFC 3339, the agent is logged and skipped. No test verifies an agent with a garbage timestamp is neither marked dead nor causes an error.

2. **TOCTOU guard** (lines 123-125): between `collect_stale_agents` and the per-agent `registry.get`, the function re-checks that the agent is still active and that `last_call_in` hasn't changed. If the agent received a new callback during the sweep window, it must NOT be marked dead. No test covers either of these guard conditions.

Without these tests a regression in the TOCTOU guard could incorrectly kill an active agent that just checked in.

**Suggested test approach**:
(1) Insert an agent with `last_call_in = "not-a-timestamp"`, sweep, assert the agent is still active and not in the returned dead list. (2) Insert an active stale agent, update its `last_call_in` after `collect_stale_agents` but before the mark step — assert it is not marked dead.
close_reason: Added coverage for malformed timestamps and stale-agent TOCTOU guard handling in liveness sweeps.

title: test: truncated DEMON_INIT payload returns error, not panic or silent corruption
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/demon.rs
**Gap type**: error path / security

`parse_init_agent` reads a sequence of fixed-size and length-prefixed fields from the decrypted DEMON_INIT payload using `read_fixed`, `read_u32_be`, `read_u64_be`, and `read_length_prefixed_*` helpers. Every helper returns `DemonProtocolError::BufferTooShort` when the remaining buffer is shorter than the requested read.

No test currently truncates an otherwise valid init packet to exercise this `BufferTooShort` path. Without a test, a refactor that accidentally bypasses the length check (e.g. using `&payload[offset..]` with an unchecked slice) would silently corrupt agent metadata or panic rather than returning a clean error.

**Suggested test approach**:
Build a valid init packet, then truncate the raw bytes at several offsets (e.g. mid-key, mid-hostname length prefix, mid-encrypted-block). Assert that `DemonPacketParser::parse` returns `Err(DemonParserError::Protocol(DemonProtocolError::BufferTooShort { .. }))` or similar for each truncation and that no agent is registered in the registry.
close_reason: Added parse_returns_buffer_too_short_for_truncated_demon_init_payload test covering mid-key, mid-IV, no-encrypted, and mid-hostname-length-prefix truncations; all assert Err(Protocol(BufferTooShort)) and no agent registration

title: test: encrypt_agent_data_at_offset lacks known-good reference vector at non-zero offset
description:
**File**: common/src/crypto.rs
**Gap type**: edge case / round-trip

The existing `encrypt_agent_data_at_offset_changes_the_keystream` test only verifies that the output at offset 3 differs from offset 0 — it does not verify the output against a *known reference value*. This means a systematic off-by-one bug in the block offset seek calculation (e.g., using `block_offset * 15` instead of `block_offset * 16`, or `block_offset - 1`) would produce a ciphertext that is still "different from offset zero" and would pass the test while silently breaking decryption compatibility with the Demon agent.

The AES-CTR block offset is security-critical: it is used to reconstruct the keystream position for a packet that arrives mid-stream. A wrong offset causes decryption garbage rather than a clean error, which is harder to diagnose in production.

**Suggested test approach**:
Pre-compute the expected ciphertext for a known key/IV/plaintext combination at block offset 2 (using an independent reference implementation or manual calculation), then assert that `encrypt_agent_data_at_offset` and `decrypt_agent_data_at_offset` both match this known value.
close_reason: Added two known-good reference vector tests for encrypt_agent_data_at_offset and decrypt_agent_data_at_offset at block_offset=2, pinning exact byte output against an independent Python reference. Also fixed duplicate test in domain.rs.

title: Implement Python API bindings for teamserver
description: In crates/teamserver: full Python API surface. RegisterCommand(name, description, callback): adds custom command visible to operators. RegisterCallback(event_type, callback): calls Python on agent events. Agent object: .task(command, args), .info (hostname, user, etc). Listener object: .start(), .stop(). Compatible with existing Havoc Python module conventions where possible. Write example module and tests.
close_reason: Implemented Havoc-compatible teamserver Python bindings, plugin command execution, listener controls, example module, and tests

title: Teamserver main-port 501 fallback leaks C2 identity in response body
description:
Introduced by: Codex <noreply@openai.com>

teamserver/src/app.rs:133-150 — agent_listener_placeholder is the Axum fallback
handler for the teamserver's main (operator) port.  Any HTTP request that does
not match /havoc or /api/v1 receives:

  HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
  agent listener endpoint not implemented yet

This plain-text response reveals that the host is a C2 teamserver, aiding
passive reconnaissance.  An external attacker who reaches the operator port (or
any redirect/CDN misconfiguration that exposes it) immediately learns the host
identity without any credentials.

The operator port should not be publicly reachable in a properly hardened
deployment, but the fallback should still return a generic 404 or a synthetic
decoy page rather than a self-identifying error.

Fix: replace the 501 + identifying string with StatusCode::NOT_FOUND and an
empty body (or a decoy 404 page matching the listener's response.body config).
close_reason: Changed the main-port fallback to a generic empty 404 and added router coverage.

title: test: add unit tests for AuditLogRepository::latest_timestamps_by_actor_for_actions
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/database.rs (AuditLogRepository, lines 1246-1270)
**Functions with no tests**:
- `fn latest_timestamps_by_actor_for_actions` (line 1246) — returns the MAX(occurred_at) per actor for a given list of action labels; the empty-actions early-return branch (line 1250) is entirely untested

**Why this matters**: This function drives the operator presence/activity dashboard. An off-by-one in the GROUP BY or MAX aggregation could return stale session timestamps without any test catching it. The early-return branch (returns empty BTreeMap when no actions supplied) is invisible to all existing tests.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Happy path: insert two audit rows for different actors with the same action label; verify each actor maps to their most recent timestamp.
2. Error path: call with empty `actions` slice; verify immediate empty BTreeMap is returned without hitting the database.
3. Edge case: single actor with multiple matching rows; verify only the latest timestamp is returned, not the first.

**Test location**: `teamserver/tests/database.rs` alongside existing audit_log_repository tests
close_reason: Added two tests: empty-actions early-return and single-actor MAX aggregation correctness

title: test: operator repository duplicate username constraint not exercised
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/database.rs
**Gap type**: error path / edge case

`operator_repository_supports_runtime_operator_crud_queries` creates one operator and reads it back. No test attempts to insert two operators with the same username. If the `ts_runtime_operators` table lacks a UNIQUE constraint (or if the upsert logic is wrong), a second create would silently overwrite or duplicate the first without error.

This matters because runtime operators are the secondary auth factor for login: a duplicate insert with a different role could silently downgrade or upgrade an operator's privileges depending on which row the query returns.

**Suggested test approach**:
Call `repository.create` twice with the same username but different `password_verifier` or role. Assert the second call returns an error (unique constraint violation). Also verify that the originally stored record is unchanged after the rejected insert.
close_reason: Added a runtime-operator duplicate-username regression test that asserts the insert fails and preserves the original row.

title: Unauthenticated WebSocket connections can idle forever before login
description:
Introduced by: Codex / Cursor Agent

teamserver/src/websocket.rs:268-280 registers every upgraded socket and immediately enters handle_authentication(), but teamserver/src/websocket.rs:400-403 then waits on socket.recv().await with no deadline. A client that upgrades the WebSocket and never sends the first login frame keeps the connection, task, and connection-manager slot alive indefinitely while bypassing the failed-login rate limiter entirely.

This is a straightforward unauthenticated resource-exhaustion path: opening many idle  sockets consumes file descriptors, memory, and task scheduling capacity without ever triggering the login failure window.

Fix: add a short authentication deadline (for example 5-10 seconds) around the first frame, close on timeout, and cover it with an integration test that opens an idle socket and asserts the server disconnects it without authenticating.
close_reason: Added a login-frame timeout for idle unauthenticated WebSocket connections and covered it with a regression test.

title: database: insert_agent_row always inserts ctr_block_offset=0 then issues a separate UPDATE
description:
Introduced by: ubuntu-htb-codex (pyct task, ae32974)

In teamserver/src/database.rs, create_with_listener_and_ctr_offset first calls insert_agent_row (which hardcodes 0_i64 for ctr_block_offset in the INSERT), then conditionally calls update_agent_ctr_block_offset if ctr_block_offset != 0. This means every non-zero initial CTR offset costs two SQL round-trips inside the same transaction instead of one.

The INSERT in insert_agent_row should bind the caller-provided ctr_block_offset directly instead of hardcoding 0, and the conditional UPDATE should be removed. The existing behaviour is functionally correct (both ops are inside a single transaction) but unnecessarily wasteful.
close_reason: Bound initial ctr_block_offset in the agent INSERT and removed the redundant follow-up update.

title: test: build_payload when the compiler process exits with non-zero status is untested
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/payload_builder.rs
**Gap type**: error path

The existing `build_payload` tests only cover the happy path (compiler exits 0 and writes an output file) and input validation rejections (wrong agent type, unsupported format, etc.). The path where the compilation tool itself fails at runtime — e.g., the compiler exits non-zero or fails to produce the output binary — is not tested.

This matters because `PayloadBuildError::ToolchainUnavailable` is a distinct error variant, and the code that handles a non-zero compiler exit is not exercised by any test. A regression where a failed compilation is silently reported as success (or where a garbage output file is read) would not be caught.

**Suggested test approach**:
Use the test-fixture pattern from `build_payload_uses_toolchain_and_returns_compiled_bytes` but write a stub compiler script that exits with code 1 (without writing an output file). Call `build_payload` and assert the result is `Err(PayloadBuildError::ToolchainUnavailable { .. })` or `Err(PayloadBuildError::InvalidRequest { .. })` depending on which error the code surfaces for a missing output file.
close_reason: Added two async tests covering the CommandFailed error path: one where the C compiler exits non-zero and one where nasm exits non-zero. Extracted setup_build_fixture helper to reduce test boilerplate.

title: test: create_operator validation error paths (empty username, empty password, duplicate) not tested
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/auth.rs
**Gap type**: error path

`AuthService::create_operator` returns three distinct errors but only the success path is tested:
- `AuthError::EmptyUsername` when username is empty/whitespace-only (line 226)
- `AuthError::EmptyPassword` when password is empty/whitespace-only (line 229)
- `AuthError::DuplicateUser` when username already exists (line 235)

These are security-relevant validation gates — a regression where empty credentials are accepted or duplicate names cause silent overwrite could be exploited.

**Suggested test approach**:
Three separate tests: (1) `create_operator("", ...)` asserts `AuthError::EmptyUsername`, (2) `create_operator("user", "  ", ...)` asserts `AuthError::EmptyPassword`, (3) create the same username twice and assert `AuthError::DuplicateUser { username: ... }`.
close_reason: Added create_operator tests for empty username, empty password, and duplicate user validation paths.

title: payload: parse compiler output for structured error reporting
description: When MinGW/NASM compilation fails, payload_builder.rs returns raw stderr text. Add a parser that extracts: filename, line number, column, error code, message from GCC/NASM error output. Return structured errors to the operator client so they can be displayed with source context. Also log structured build errors to the audit trail.
close_reason: Added CompilerDiagnostic type to common, parse_compiler_diagnostic parser for GCC/NASM stderr in payload_builder, CommandFailed now carries diagnostics, websocket handler re-emits diagnostics and logs payload.build audit entries with structured error data

title: teamserver: per-IP rate limiting on DEMON_INIT registration
description: The agent registry flood (red-cell-c2-2ne) tracks a size cap but not rate limiting. Add a per-source-IP rate limiter on DEMON_INIT acceptance, separate from the operator login rate limiter. Configurable: max_inits_per_minute_per_ip (suggested default: 10). Use a sliding window similar to LoginRateLimiter. Apply in http_listener_handler before any packet parsing.
close_reason: DemonInitRateLimiter in listeners.rs: sliding window, max 5 inits/60s per IP, 10k IP cap, applied before packet parsing

title: Client Python command dispatcher lowercases script arguments
description:
Introduced by: Codex

`crates/client/src/python.rs:221-243` normalizes the entire console input with `normalize_command_name()` before splitting off the registered command name. That lowercases every argument and the reconstructed `CommandContext.command_line`, so Havoc-compatible client scripts now receive `C:\Temp\Foo.txt` as `c:\temp\foo.txt`, mixed-case passwords/tokens in lowercase, etc.

Correct behavior: only command-name matching should be case-insensitive; the unmatched argument tail and `command_line` passed to callbacks must preserve the operators original casing exactly.
close_reason: Preserved original command_line and argument casing while keeping command-name matching case-insensitive.

title: test: operator_agent_info with no pivot parent or children is not tested
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/agent_events.rs
**Gap type**: edge case

`operator_agent_info` builds pivot metadata from a `PivotInfo`:
```rust
let parent = pivots.parent.map(|agent_id| format!("{agent_id:08X}"));
let links = pivots.children.iter()...;
...
pivot_parent: parent.unwrap_or_default(),
```
Both existing tests (`operator_agent_info_preserves_parent_and_child_pivots` and `agent_new_event_uses_shared_operator_agent_info`) supply a non-None parent. The parentless case — where `pivot_parent` must be an empty string and `pivots.parent` must be `None` — is never tested.

A regression that changes `unwrap_or_default()` to `unwrap_or("null"))` would pass all current tests but break the Havoc operator protocol.

**Suggested test approach**:
Call `operator_agent_info("http", magic, &agent, &PivotInfo { parent: None, children: vec![] })` and assert `info.pivot_parent.is_empty()` and `info.pivots.parent.is_none()`.
close_reason: Added test operator_agent_info_with_no_parent_or_children_leaves_pivot_fields_empty asserting pivot_parent is empty and pivots.parent is None when PivotInfo has no parent or children

title: arch: duplicate HttpListenerConfig structs in config.rs and domain.rs
description:
Introduced by: Codex <noreply@openai.com>

common/src/config.rs:475 and common/src/domain.rs:100

## What is wrong

There are two distinct `pub struct HttpListenerConfig` definitions in the common crate:

- `common::config::HttpListenerConfig` (config.rs:475) — profile-parsing shape used by the
  YAOTL parser, with profile-specific field names (PortBind, HostBind, etc.)
- `common::domain::HttpListenerConfig` (domain.rs:100) — runtime domain type used by the
  teamserver and re-exported from `common::lib`

Both are re-exported through different paths. The types have overlapping fields but different
serialization layouts, which creates ambiguity at call sites and makes it easy to accidentally
import the wrong type.

## Why it matters

Architecture decision in AGENTS.md states shared types live in `./common`. Having two types
with identical names for the same conceptual entity in the same crate is a code smell that will
cause import confusion and maintenance drift. Future agents editing one type won't notice the
other, leading to inconsistent field sets.

## Fix

Rename `common::config::HttpListenerConfig` to `ProfileHttpListenerConfig` or merge the two
structs into one with serde rename annotations to handle both shapes. Ensure only one
HttpListenerConfig type lives in common.
close_reason: Renamed common::config::HttpListenerConfig to ProfileHttpListenerConfig to remove the duplicate HttpListenerConfig type from red-cell-common.

title: test: add integration test for HTTP listener behind_redirector IP attribution
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/http_listener_pipeline.rs
**Gap type**: error path / security-relevant behavior

The existing test `http_listener_pipeline_registers_agent_and_broadcasts_checkin` sets an `x-real-ip` header but uses `behind_redirector: false`, correctly verifying that the header is ignored. However, there is no test for `behind_redirector: true` where `x-real-ip` IS used for IP attribution.

If the reverse-proxy IP extraction code regresses, all agent external IPs are silently misattributed — the teamserver would log the reverse proxy's IP instead of the real agent IP with no visible error. This matters for incident response accuracy.

Also missing: a test that the `x-real-ip` header is only respected when the connection originates from a configured `trusted_proxy_peers` address (relates to red-cell-c2-9eac). An agent behind a redirector can currently spoof attribution by forging `x-real-ip` unless trusted-peer enforcement is implemented and tested.

**Suggested test approach**:
Spin up an HTTP listener with `behind_redirector: true` and `trusted_proxy_peers: ["127.0.0.1"]`. Send a demon INIT body with an `x-real-ip: 198.51.100.44` header and assert that `stored.external_ip == "198.51.100.44"`. As a second case, use `trusted_proxy_peers` that does NOT include the connection peer IP and assert the header is ignored.
close_reason: Added HTTP listener integration coverage for trusted and untrusted redirector x-real-ip attribution.

title: LoginRateLimiter windows map grows unboundedly under IPs never seen again
description:
Introduced by: Cursor Agent <noreply@cursor.com>

crates/teamserver/src/websocket.rs — LoginRateLimiter (commit 1aa5dd6)

The LoginRateLimiter::windows HashMap is pruned lazily: expired entries are only removed when the same IP makes a follow-up request (is_allowed/record_failure). An adversary probing from many distinct source IPs—or a coordinated DoS scan—inserts one LoginAttemptWindow per IP and those entries are never evicted without a second request from the same IP. Over time the operator-facing port accumulates O(attacker IPs) entries with no bound.

This is the same class of bug as red-cell-c2-pkl (AgentSocketState) and red-cell-c2-aow (DownloadTracker). Even though the operator port is not publicly advertised, the teamserver may be reachable from monitored network ranges and this provides a steady memory-exhaustion path.

Fix: add a max-size eviction pass (similar to evict_oldest_contexts in agents.rs) in record_failure, OR launch a background tokio::spawn task that sweeps and removes expired windows every LOGIN_WINDOW_DURATION. Cap total map size at, e.g., 10,000 entries.
close_reason: Pruned expired login windows and capped tracked IP entries in LoginRateLimiter.

title: AES-256-CTR IV reused for every agent packet — keystream reuse
description:
Introduced by: Codex

crates/teamserver/src/demon.rs:154 and crates/teamserver/src/dispatch.rs:462–470

The same key+IV pair stored at DEMON_INIT is reused as-is for every
subsequent encrypt/decrypt call for a given agent.  AES-CTR mode is a
stream cipher: it XORs each plaintext byte with a keystream derived from
(key, counter-starting-at-IV).  Reusing the same IV means the exact same
keystream is generated for every message.

Result: for any two ciphertexts C1 and C2 from the same agent session,
C1 XOR C2 = P1 XOR P2.  An observer who knows one plaintext (e.g. the
deterministic ACK body) can immediately recover the other.

Worst case is with Secure=false listeners where TLS is absent: the
keystream-reuse attack has no outer layer to stop it.

Fix: advance a per-session counter stored alongside the key/IV in
AgentEncryptionInfo, or generate a fresh nonce per message and prepend it
to the ciphertext.  Verify the chosen approach matches the Demon C agent's
counter-advance behavior before shipping.
close_reason: Per-agent AES-CTR block counter tracking implemented. ctr_block_offset in AgentEntry, encrypt_for_agent/decrypt_from_agent on AgentRegistry, updated init/callback/job flows in demon.rs+dispatch.rs+listeners.rs. All 326 tests passing, clippy clean, formatted.

title: SOCKS relay write_client_data failure silently dropped in socket callback handler
description:
Introduced by: Codex

crates/teamserver/src/dispatch.rs:2955

In the DemonSocketCommand::Read branch:
  let _ = sockets.write_client_data(agent_id, socket_id, &data).await;

Errors from write_client_data (broken pipe, unknown socket ID, relay
task exited) are completely discarded.  The SOCKS client that sent the
request never receives the data and receives no error signal.  The
operator session has no visibility into the delivery failure.

This contrasts with the error handling directly above (lines 2942–2950)
which does broadcast an operator error event when the agent reports a read
failure.

Fix: log a warning (or broadcast an operator error event) when
write_client_data returns Err, consistent with the error path immediately
above it.
close_reason: Dispatcher now logs and broadcasts reverse-proxy relay delivery failures, with regression coverage.

title: Zero-key bypass in DEMON_INIT allows unauthenticated plaintext agent registration
description:
Introduced by: Codex

crates/teamserver/src/demon.rs:253 — parse_init_agent() treats any DEMON_INIT packet where the 32-byte AES key field is all-zeros as a plaintext payload (no decryption). Any process that can reach the agent-facing HTTP/SMB listener port can craft a valid DEMON_INIT with a zeroed key, register as an agent without possessing the pre-shared implant key, and receive a plaintext init-ack.

crates/teamserver/src/demon.rs:179 — build_init_ack() mirrors this: if the stored key is all-zeros, the ack (containing the agent_id) is returned as plaintext, confirming the handshake for a zero-key 'agent'.

The bypass is inherited from the Havoc debug/test code path and is intentional for development convenience, but it exists unchanged in production code. A threat actor who can reach the listener endpoint (e.g. because the listener is internet-exposed for real agents) can use it to register phantom agents without a properly-keyed implant binary.

Fix: reject DEMON_INIT packets with an all-zero key in production builds, or gate the bypass behind an explicit profile-level debug flag (e.g. Demon { AllowPlaintextInit = false }). At minimum, log a prominent warning when a zero-key init is accepted.
close_reason: Rejected plaintext zero-key DEMON_INITs are covered at the HTTP listener boundary and never register agents.

title: add_bytes silently emits zero-length prefix on slice > 4 GiB instead of returning error
description: In crates/teamserver/src/payload_builder.rs at line 1088, the helper 'add_bytes' calls 'u32::try_from(value.len()).unwrap_or_default()'. If the slice ever exceeds u32::MAX (~4 GiB), the length prefix is silently written as 0, corrupting the packed config binary stream rather than propagating an error. Should propagate a PayloadBuildError::InvalidRequest instead. In practice payloads are never this large, but the silent truncation is an incorrect invariant: the code should panic or return Err, never silently proceed with corrupt data.
close_reason: add_bytes and add_wstring now return Result<(), PayloadBuildError>; slice length exceeding u32::MAX returns InvalidRequest instead of silently writing 0

title: test: add repository coverage for runtime operator password updates and listener state transitions
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/database.rs
**Functions with no tests**:
- fn update_password_verifier (line 596) — replaces the stored password verifier for a runtime operator.
- fn set_state (line 708) — updates listener runtime state fields such as status and last_error without rewriting the full listener config.

**Why this matters**: these are database write paths for authentication and listener lifecycle state. If they regress, runtime operators can become unable to log in after normalization, and listeners can report stale Running/Stopped/Error state to operators and automation.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Happy path: update_password_verifier changes the persisted verifier and set_state updates status plus last_error as expected.
2. Error path: updating a missing operator or listener leaves the database unchanged and reports the expected repository behavior.
3. Edge case: set_state clears last_error when passed None and preserves the static listener configuration fields.

**Test location**: teamserver/tests/database.rs
close_reason: Added repository integration coverage for operator password verifier updates and listener runtime state transitions.

title: refactor: extract shared test helpers from duplicate integration test files
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/e2e_operator_agent_session.rs, teamserver/tests/mock_demon_agent_checkin.rs
**Gap type**: quality / maintainability

`e2e_operator_agent_session.rs` and `mock_demon_agent_checkin.rs` contain nearly identical implementations of several test helper functions:
- `login()`
- `read_operator_message()`
- `read_operator_snapshot()`
- `available_port_excluding()`
- `wait_for_listener()`
- `valid_demon_init_body()`
- `valid_demon_callback_body()`
- `add_length_prefixed_bytes_be()` / `add_length_prefixed_utf16_be()`

This means that any protocol change (e.g., new metadata fields in the demon init body or new websocket framing) requires updating both files. The duplication has already caused the two files to diverge slightly (e.g., minor differences in the `login` helper and how `error_for_status` is called). A bug introduced during copy-paste could cause one test to silently accept malformed output that the other rejects.

**Suggested approach**:
Create `teamserver/tests/common/mod.rs` with shared helpers and import them via `mod common;` in each test file. This also makes it easier to add future integration tests without re-implementing the full protocol harness.
close_reason: Created teamserver/tests/common/mod.rs with all duplicated helpers (login, login_as, read_operator_message, read_operator_snapshot, available_port_excluding, wait_for_listener, valid_demon_init_body, valid_demon_callback_body, add_length_prefixed_bytes_be/utf16_be, command_output_payload, spawn_test_server, assert_no_operator_message). Both test files updated to use mod common; All tests pass.

title: audit: unknown agent callback not recorded in audit log
description:
Introduced by: Michel Klomp

teamserver/src/listeners.rs ~line 1378 — process_demon_transport dispatches ParsedDemonPacket::Callback packets; when decrypt_from_agent returns AgentNotFound the error is only logged at warn! level and no audit record is written. Contrast with ParsedDemonPacket::Reconnect at ~line 1362 where record_unknown_reconnect_probe is called. An attacker can repeatedly probe the listener with unknown agent IDs to fingerprint or DoS the registry with no forensic evidence.

Fix: add a record_unknown_callback_probe call in the AgentNotFound error arm, mirroring the reconnect path.
close_reason: Added record_unknown_callback_probe in the AgentNotFound error arm of process_demon_transport, mirroring the reconnect probe path. Returns Fake404 and writes an agent.callback_probe audit entry. Covered by a new end-to-end test.

title: SMB DEMON_INIT rate limiter uses hardcoded LOCALHOST — per-IP limit non-functional for all SMB
description: In teamserver/src/listeners.rs, handle_smb_connection() assigns client_ip = IpAddr::V4(Ipv4Addr::LOCALHOST) for every incoming SMB connection before passing to DemonInitRateLimiter. This means all SMB DEMON_INIT attempts are bucketed under 127.0.0.1; the per-IP limit (5 attempts / 60 s) is shared across every SMB agent and fires after 5 total SMB DEMON_INIT requests regardless of source, while simultaneously allowing unlimited per-true-IP bypasses via parallel SMB connections. Introduced in commit 8d10163 (Co-Authored-By: Codex). Fix: extract the actual peer address from the Unix/SMB socket context (or use a synthetic IP derived from agent_id/pipe name) before passing to the rate limiter.
close_reason: derive per-agent synthetic IPv4 from frame.agent_id.to_be_bytes() in handle_smb_connection; each agent_id now gets its own rate-limit bucket instead of sharing 127.0.0.1; updated all affected tests

title: Operator credentials are stored as bare SHA3-256 digests
description:
Introduced by: Michel Klomp

teamserver/src/auth.rs:165 hashes profile passwords with hash_password_sha3(), and teamserver/src/auth.rs:261-267 persists runtime-created operators with the same unsalted SHA3-256 digest. The stored value is then used directly as the login verifier.

Why it matters: a database leak immediately yields fast, unsalted password digests that can be cracked offline. The current constant-time compare only protects online verification; it does not protect the persisted credential material. Because the client already submits SHA3(password) over the Havoc-compatible login protocol, the server can harden storage without breaking wire compatibility by stretching or keying that submitted digest before persistence.

Fix: store a server-side verifier such as Argon2id(hash_password_sha3(password)) or an equivalent keyed KDF output for runtime operators, and use the same hardened verifier for profile-loaded credentials in memory. Add a migration path for existing persisted runtime operators so the database no longer stores bare SHA3 digests.
close_reason: Stored Argon2id verifiers for operator SHA3 digests, upgraded legacy runtime rows on load, and added auth migration coverage

title: build_dns_txt_response hardcodes TXT QTYPE in question section regardless of original query type
description:
In listeners.rs, build_dns_txt_response reconstructs the question section of the DNS response by writing qname_raw followed by DNS_TYPE_TXT (16) and DNS_CLASS_IN. However, the original query's QTYPE is not passed in, so if the query was an A record query (QTYPE=1), the response question section claims QTYPE=TXT (16), which violates RFC 1035 §4.1.2 (the question section in a response must echo the original question).

The ParsedDnsQuery struct already contains a qtype field. The fix is to add a qtype parameter to build_dns_txt_response (or pass the full ParsedDnsQuery) and use query.qtype instead of DNS_TYPE_TXT when writing the question section.

Priority 3 — this is a protocol correctness issue but doesn't affect C2 functionality since all C2 traffic uses TXT queries, and the current code always responds with a TXT answer regardless.
close_reason: Preserved the original DNS query QTYPE when rebuilding TXT response questions and added a regression test.

title: test: authenticate_message with completely invalid JSON returns InvalidMessageJson error
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/auth.rs
**Gap type**: error path

The `authenticate_message` method has two distinct error paths:
1. Valid JSON but non-Login message → `Err(AuthError::InvalidLoginMessage)`
2. Invalid/unparseable JSON → `Err(AuthError::InvalidMessageJson(...))`

Path 1 is tested in `authenticate_message_rejects_non_login_messages`, but path 2 is completely untested. Without a test, a future refactor could silently collapse both paths into the same error or accidentally panic on malformed input — a security-relevant regression since this is the outer gate for operator connections.

**Suggested test approach**:
Call `authenticate_message` with an input like `"not-valid-json{"` and assert the result is `Err(AuthError::InvalidMessageJson(_))`. Confirm no session is created and no panic occurs.
close_reason: Added authenticate_message_rejects_invalid_json test: passes invalid JSON and asserts Err(InvalidMessageJson(_)) with no session created

title: test: add unit coverage for AuditResultStatus::as_str
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/audit.rs
**Functions with no tests**:
- `fn as_str` (line 27) — returns the stable serialized status label used when building audit filters and payloads.

**Why this matters**: Audit status filtering depends on a stable string mapping. A regression here could silently break `result_status` filtering or serialized webhook/database output without failing broader integration tests.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Happy path: `AuditResultStatus::Success.as_str()` returns `"success"`.
2. Happy path: `AuditResultStatus::Failure.as_str()` returns `"failure"`.
3. Edge case: assert the mapping is lowercase and stable for both enum variants so future renames cannot change the wire value accidentally.

**Test location**: inline `#[cfg(test)]` in `teamserver/src/audit.rs`
close_reason: Added three inline unit tests: as_str_success, as_str_failure, and as_str_is_lowercase_and_stable covering both enum variants and wire-label stability.

title: test: agent_mark_event Alive case not covered — only Dead path is tested
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/agent_events.rs
**Gap type**: edge case

`agent_mark_event` returns `"Alive"` when `agent.active == true` and `"Dead"` when false. The existing test `agent_mark_event_uses_dead_status_for_inactive_agents` covers only the Dead branch. The Alive branch is untested.

This is a simple two-branch function and the Alive path is the one broadcast when an agent that was previously considered dead reconnects — a regression here could silently mark a recovered agent as Dead in operator consoles.

**Suggested test approach**:
Call `agent_mark_event` with an agent where `active = true`, assert the resulting `OperatorMessage::AgentUpdate` has `marked == "Alive"`.
close_reason: Added unit coverage for the Alive branch in agent_mark_event.

title: consistency: DemonInitRateLimiter and LoginRateLimiter duplicate eviction logic verbatim
description:
Introduced by: Codex <noreply@openai.com> (rate limiter implementation)

teamserver/src/listeners.rs and teamserver/src/websocket.rs both implement a sliding-window rate limiter with identical eviction logic: MAX_*_ATTEMPT_WINDOWS = 10_000, evict_oldest_* functions that remove the oldest half of entries when the cap is reached.

The duplicated code means a bug fix or policy change in one eviction path will not automatically apply to the other. Both currently use the same cap and strategy with no documented reason for the divergence.

Fix: extract a generic SlidingWindowRateLimiter<K> type (or at minimum a shared evict_oldest_windows() utility function) into a shared module and have both usages delegate to it.
close_reason: extracted AttemptWindow, evict_oldest_windows, and prune_expired_windows into teamserver/src/rate_limiter.rs; both DemonInitRateLimiter and LoginRateLimiter now delegate to the shared functions

title: test: add constructor coverage for PayloadBuilderService::from_profile
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/payload_builder.rs
**Functions with no tests**:
- `fn from_profile` (line 149) — resolves the MinGW/NASM toolchain and required Havoc payload asset paths from the loaded profile.

**Why this matters**: this is the public constructor for payload building. If it accepts a broken toolchain configuration or mis-resolves repository assets, payload generation will fail at runtime after operator requests are already in flight.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Happy path: resolve a profile whose build settings point to usable executables and existing payload assets.
2. Error path: reject a profile with a missing compiler or NASM binary and assert `PayloadBuildError::ToolchainUnavailable`.
3. Edge case: reject missing Havoc payload asset files even when the toolchain paths are valid.

**Test location**: inline `#[cfg(test)]` in `teamserver/src/payload_builder.rs`
close_reason: Added constructor coverage for successful toolchain resolution, missing binaries, and missing Havoc payload assets.

title: test: load_tls_identity error paths (missing cert, missing key, corrupt PEM) not tested
description:
**File**: common/src/tls.rs
**Gap type**: error path

`load_tls_identity` returns three error variants that have no test coverage:
- `TlsError::MissingCertificates` when the PEM buffer contains no X.509 certificate blocks
- `TlsError::MissingPrivateKey` when the PEM buffer contains no private key block
- `TlsError::Pem` when the PEM data is completely corrupt/binary

`load_tls_identity_from_files` also has an untested `TlsError::ReadFile` path (non-existent paths). The existing round-trip test only exercises the happy path.

Since TLS identity loading is on the critical path for listener startup and a malformed cert file should produce a clear error rather than a panic or silent failure, these gaps matter.

**Suggested test approach**:
Pass empty bytes to `load_tls_identity` (→ MissingCertificates), pass a valid cert PEM but empty key (→ MissingPrivateKey), call `load_tls_identity_from_files` with a non-existent path (→ ReadFile), and assert the correct `TlsError` variant in each case.
close_reason: Added TLS identity loader tests for missing cert, missing key, corrupt PEM, and missing file errors.

title: handle_token_callback returns error for all subcommands except Impersonate
description: crates/teamserver/src/dispatch.rs handle_token_callback (registered for COMMAND_TOKEN) only handles DemonTokenCommand::Impersonate and propagates a CommandDispatchError for every other subcommand (Steal=2, List=3, PrivsGetOrList=4, Make=5, GetUid=6, Revert=7, Remove=8, Clear=9, FindTokens=10). When the Demon agent sends back a callback for any of those subcommands, the handler fails, causing the response to be silently dropped (the error is logged but no operator event is emitted). Either implement the remaining subcommands or handle them with a generic/no-op response rather than returning an error that kills the callback.
close_reason: Implemented all token subcommand callbacks (Steal, List, PrivsGetOrList, Make, GetUid, Revert, Remove, Clear, FindTokens) with protocol-compatible parsing matching the Havoc Demon agent. Also updated encode_token_payload and token_subcommand to support all subcommands from the operator side. Added 12 tests covering all subcommands.

title: test: deserialize_bool_from_any rejects unrecognized string values like "yes" or "maybe"
description:
**File**: common/src/domain.rs
**Gap type**: error path

`deserialize_bool_from_any` accepts the strings `"true"`, `"1"`, `"false"`, `"0"`, and `""` — all other strings fall into the error branch: `Err(de::Error::custom(format!("invalid boolean value `{other}`")))`. This branch has no test coverage.

There is an existing open issue (red-cell-c2-3rio) for the *integer* case (values outside {0,1}), but the *string* case is distinct. Fields like `active` and `elevated` on `AgentRecord` use this deserializer. If a malformed Havoc message arrived with a value like `"yes"` or `"Active"`, the deserializer should cleanly reject it rather than silently produce a wrong value.

**Suggested test approach**:
Call `serde_json::from_value::<AgentRecord>` (or directly test the helper via a JSON-level test) with `"Active": "yes"` or `"Elevated": "maybe"` and assert that deserialization fails with an error message containing `"invalid boolean value"`.
close_reason: Added two tests: rejects 'yes' on Active and 'maybe' on Elevated, both asserting error message contains 'invalid boolean value'

title: Auth Argon2 hashing in test profile setup makes full test suite unfeasibly slow
description:
Introduced by: Codex <noreply@openai.com>

teamserver/src/auth.rs:156-163 — AuthService::from_profile now calls password_verifier_for_sha3 (which runs Argon2::default().hash_password) for every configured profile operator plus the dummy verifier. Previously this was a single fast SHA3 call.

Every test that calls AuthService::from_profile in its setup now incurs N+1 Argon2 hashes (N profile operators + 1 dummy). The standard test profile has 2 operators, so each from_profile call does 3 Argon2 operations. At 19MB memory cost and 2 iterations per hash, this adds ~1-2 seconds per test setup. Auth, API, websocket, rbac, and main tests all call from_profile, meaning hundreds of Argon2 operations run during the full suite.

Observed: cargo test --workspace with all 360 tests times out consistently (>120s). Individual module filters pass (auth: 23s for 18 tests, api: 8s for 50 tests with 4 threads), but running the entire suite is impractical.

Correct behavior: Test setups should use a fast in-memory credential path (e.g., pre-computed test verifiers via once_cell/lazy_static, or a test-only constructor that skips Argon2 and stores a sentinel that compare matches). Production from_profile should remain hardened.
close_reason: Added cfg(test) OnceLock cache in password_verifier_for_sha3: each unique SHA3 input is hashed via Argon2 at most once per test process. Full suite now completes in ~16s runtime vs previous timeout >120s. Production path unchanged.

title: DNS listener response map (DnsPendingResponse) has no expiry, causing unbounded memory growth
description:
In listeners.rs, DnsListenerState contains a 'responses: Mutex<HashMap<u32, DnsPendingResponse>>' that is populated when an agent upload is processed successfully. Entries are never removed: handle_download returns 'done' when the agent has polled all chunks but does not call responses.remove(&agent_id). Under active C2 use, every completed agent interaction leaves a stale entry in this map.

By contrast, the uploads map does expire entries via 'uploads.retain(|_, p| p.received_at.elapsed().as_secs() < DNS_UPLOAD_TIMEOUT_SECS)' called in try_assemble_upload.

Fix: either (a) remove the entry from responses inside handle_download when idx >= total (converting &self to &mut self or using interior mutability properly), or (b) add a similar time-based expiry by adding a received_at timestamp to DnsPendingResponse and purging in handle_download or a background cleanup task.
close_reason: Removed completed DNS pending responses on terminal download polls and added a regression test.

title: No end-to-end integration test: HTTP listener → parser → dispatcher pipeline
description:
Introduced by: Codex <noreply@openai.com>

crates/teamserver/tests/ contains only database.rs. There is no integration test that exercises the full path: HTTP listener receives a Demon packet → DemonPacketParser parses it → CommandDispatcher routes the callback → operator event is broadcast.

All components are unit-tested in isolation, but a wiring error (e.g., the listener passing the wrong field as external_ip, or the dispatcher failing to find the agent) would not be caught.

Fix: add an integration test that starts a real HTTP listener on a loopback port, sends a synthesized DEMON_INIT followed by a COMMAND_CHECKIN, and asserts that the agent appears in the registry and the correct operator event is broadcast.
close_reason: Added a real HTTP listener integration test covering DEMON_INIT registration, registry state, and COMMAND_CHECKIN event dispatch.

title: test: BOF_CALLBACK_ERROR (0x0d) sub-type in handle_inline_execute_callback is the only untested BOF path
description:
File: teamserver/src/dispatch/assembly.rs
Gap type: error path / edge case

The handle_inline_execute_callback function dispatches 6 BOF sub-types. Five are covered by the inline_execute_bof_output_broadcasts_agent_response test in dispatch/mod.rs (BOF_CALLBACK_OUTPUT=0x00, BOF_EXCEPTION=1, BOF_SYMBOL_NOT_FOUND=2, BOF_RAN_OK=3, BOF_COULD_NOT_RUN=4). The BOF_CALLBACK_ERROR sub-type (0x0d) — standard error text output from a BOF — has no test.

This means a regression in error output parsing (e.g., reading the wrong number of bytes for the error string length) would produce silent garbage in operator consoles without any test catching it. The 0x0d constant value is also non-obvious and a transcription error would be invisible without a test.

Suggested test approach:
Build a payload with sub-type 0x0d followed by a length-prefixed error string, dispatch it through CommandDispatcher::with_builtin_handlers, and assert the broadcast event has Type=Error and the expected message content.

title: parse_checkin_metadata silently saturates working_hours to i32::MAX on overflow
description:
Introduced by: Codex (commit 983789a - fix: refresh metadata on demon checkin)
